summary: Gruber argues that the real problem with Mac OS X's brushed metal window theme isn't whether it looks good, but that Apple uses it inconsistently, violating its own Human Interface Guidelines. He distinguishes between the superficial aesthetic debate and the deeper consistency argument, noting that the Mac's usability advantage has historically depended on apps looking and feeling uniform. Safari's adoption of brushed metal despite fitting none of the HIG's criteria was a watershed moment proving Apple ignores its own rules. Gruber contends the HIG should either be enforced or rewritten to reflect Apple's actual capricious policy, because a guidelines document that doesn't match reality has no credibility.
key_points: The brushed metal debate has two distinct layers: personal aesthetic preference and the deeper issue of UI consistency across the platform
The behavioral difference of metal windows (drag from anywhere) makes inconsistency worse — if it's a good idea, all windows should support it
Safari was the watershed moment where Apple clearly ignored its own HIG criteria for brushed metal usage
The HIG itself undermines consistency by framing the choice in aesthetic terms ('too heavy') rather than emphasizing strict consistency rules
If Apple won't follow the HIG, the HIG should be rewritten to honestly reflect actual policy — guidelines are only credible when they match practice
money_quotes: The HIG states, 'Don't use the brushed metal look indiscriminately', but indiscriminate is precisely the word to describe Apple's use of it.
A large part of the Mac's historical usability advantage is that Mac applications all look and feel the same.
If it's a good usability idea, all windows should work like this; otherwise, none should.
It's quite obvious that the one and only reason Safari uses brushed metal is that someone at Apple thinks it looks better that way.
The HIG is only credible if it accurately reflects Apple's actual policy. If the policy isn't going to change, then the HIG should.
To those of you who think this state of affairs is just fine, I ask this: If two themes are OK, why not three or four?
key_insight: Platform guidelines that the platform owner itself ignores are worse than no guidelines at all, because they undermine the credibility of the entire consistency framework.
